title: Allocate minimum pages (binary search answer)
description: Allocate books to minimize maximum pages using binary search

examples.explanation: We need to partition [10,20,30,40] into 2 contiguous groups. Explore all partitions: 1. Split after index 0: Group1=[10], sum=10; Group2=[20,30,40], sum=90; max=90. 2. Split after index 1: Group1=[10,20], sum=30; Group2=[30,40], sum=70; max=70. 3. Split after index 2: Group1=[10,20,30], sum=60; Group2=[40], sum=40; max=60. Minimum max is 60.

Partition [12,34,67,90] into 2 groups. Partitions: 1. Split after 0: [12] sum=12, [34,67,90] sum=191, max=191. 2. Split after 1: [12,34] sum=46, [67,90] sum=157, max=157. 3. Split after 2: [12,34,67] sum=113, [90] sum=90, max=113. Minimum max is 113.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(k * n^k)
approaches.summary: Try all possible ways to partition the books into k contiguous groups using recursion, calculating the maximum pages per student for each partition. Use this when understanding the problem's combinatorial nature, but it's impractical for large inputs due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Allocation of Minimum Pages
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution trying all possible partitions of books into k contiguous groups to minimize maximum pages per student.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the book allocation problem. The intuition is that we need to explore all possible ways to split the array of page counts into exactly k contiguous groups, where each group represents books assigned to one student. For each partition, we calculate the maximum sum of pages in any group (which represents the maximum load for any student), and we want to minimize this maximum. The brute force approach generates all possible partitions by recursively deciding where to place dividers between books. At each step in the recursion, we decide how many books to assign to the current student (from at least 1 book up to the remaining books), then recursively assign the remaining books to the remaining students. The base case occurs when we have only one student left—they must take all remaining books. We track the maximum pages for the current partition and update the global minimum. The time complexity is O(k * n^k) because there are roughly n choose (k-1) ways to place dividers, and for each partition we compute sums in O(k) time. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This approach helps understand the problem's combinatorial nature but is impractical for large n due to exponential growth. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete recursive function that calls the helper from L1 to compute partition sums. Common mistakes include not handling the base case correctly (e.g., when students > books), not ensuring contiguous groups, or inefficiently recomputing sums. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function that takes current index, remaining students, and current maximum, exploring all splits and updating a global answer.

examples.trace: We need to partition [10,20,30,40] into 2 contiguous groups. Explore all partitions: 1. Split after index 0: Group1=[10], sum=10; Group2=[20,30,40], sum=90; max=90. 2. Split after index 1: Group1=[10,20], sum=30; Group2=[30,40], sum=70; max=70. 3. Split after index 2: Group1=[10,20,30], sum=60; Group2=[40], sum=40; max=60. Minimum max is 60.

Partition [12,34,67,90] into 2 groups. Partitions: 1. Split after 0: [12] sum=12, [34,67,90] sum=191, max=191. 2. Split after 1: [12,34] sum=46, [67,90] sum=157, max=157. 3. Split after 2: [12,34,67] sum=113, [90] sum=90, max=113. Minimum max is 113.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Partition Generator with Sum Calculation
ladders.desc: Recursive function that generates all partitions and computes maximum sum for each.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive helper function that implements the brute force exploration. The intuition is to treat the problem as placing k-1 dividers between n books to form k contiguous groups. We recursively decide how many books to assign to the current student: starting from the current index in the pages array, we try assigning 1 book, then 2 books, etc., up to the remaining books minus the students left. For each choice, we compute the sum of pages for the current student's books, update the current maximum (the max of this sum and previous maximum), and recursively assign the remaining books to the remaining students. The base case is when only one student remains: they must take all remaining books, so we compute the final sum and update the global minimum of maximums. The function returns the minimum maximum pages found. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(k * n^k) in the worst case as it explores all combinations. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution in L0 with initial parameters. Connection to L2: It relies on the combinatorial insight from L2. Common mistakes include incorrect bounds for the loop (e.g., not leaving enough books for remaining students), not handling the base case properly, or inefficient sum computation. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function min_max_pages(idx, students_left, curr_max) that loops over possible end indices, computes sum from idx to end, and recurses.

examples.input: pages = [10, 20], k = 2, start at idx=0, students=2
examples.trace: We have 2 students and 2 books. For student 1: Try assign 1 book: sum=10, curr_max=10, recurse with idx=1, students=1. Base case: student 2 gets [20], sum=20, max(10,20)=20, update global min=20. Backtrack. Try assign 2 books: invalid because no books left for student 2. So min max is 20.

examples.input: pages = [5, 10, 15], k = 2, start at idx=0, students=2
examples.trace: For student 1: Assign 1 book: sum=5, curr_max=5, recurse idx=1, students=1. Base: student 2 gets [10,15], sum=25, max=25, global min=25. Assign 2 books: sum=15, curr_max=15, recurse idx=2, students=1. Base: student 2 gets [15], sum=15, max=15, update global min=15. Minimum is 15? Wait, check: Actually partitions: [5] and [10,15] gives max 25; [5,10] and [15] gives max 15. So output should be 15. Correction: trace shows global min becomes 15.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Combinatorial Partitioning into Contiguous Groups
ladders.desc: The insight that we need to try all ways to split the array into k contiguous segments.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central combinatorial insight required for the brute force approach. The intuition is that allocating books to k students such that each gets a contiguous set of books is equivalent to placing k-1 dividers between the n books. For example, with n=4 books and k=2 students, we can place a divider after book 1, after book 2, or after book 3—giving three possible partitions. In general, the number of ways is C(n-1, k-1), which grows exponentially. The core logic involves systematically generating all these partitions. We can think of it as recursively deciding where to cut the array: for the first student, assign books from start to some end index, then recursively process the remaining books for the remaining students. This ensures contiguity. The step-by-step approach: Start with the entire array and k students. For the current segment, try all possible end positions that leave at least one book per remaining student. For each, partition the current segment and recurse. The base case is when k=1: assign all remaining books. This logic directly leads to the recursive implementation. Time complexity arises from the number of partitions, which is exponential. Space is O(n) for recursion. Connection to L1: This insight is implemented in the recursive function of L1. Connection to L3: It builds upon array slicing from L3. Common mistakes include generating non-contiguous partitions or missing some partitions due to incorrect bounds. Real code pattern: Use recursion with a loop over end indices, ensuring at least (students_left - 1) books remain.

examples.trace: Books indexed 0,1,2. Place 1 divider. Options: after index 0: group1=[0], group2=[1,2]; after index 1: group1=[0,1], group2=[2]. That's all partitions.

Need 2 dividers. Place first divider after index 0: then remaining [1,2,3] with k=2. For that, partitions: [1|23] and [12|3]. So overall: [0|1|23] and [0|12|3]. Place first divider after index 1: remaining [2,3] with k=2, partition: [2|3]. Overall: [01|2|3]. Total 3 partitions.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Sum of Contiguous Subarray
ladders.desc: Efficiently calculate the sum of pages between two indices in the array.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses a fundamental operation needed in the brute force approach: computing the sum of a contiguous segment of the pages array. In the recursive solution, whenever we assign a set of books to a student, we need to know the total pages for that segment to update the current maximum. The intuition is that we can compute this sum by iterating from the start index to the end index and adding up the page counts. For an array pages of length n, the sum from index i to j inclusive is pages[i] + pages[i+1] + ... + pages[j]. This is a basic linear-time operation. However, in the brute force approach, we might recompute sums multiple times, leading to inefficiency—but since the approach is already exponential, we accept this. The step-by-step algorithm: Given array pages, start index idx, and end index end (inclusive), initialize sum=0, loop from i=idx to end, add pages[i] to sum, return sum. Time complexity is O(length of segment), which in worst case is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This sum computation is used when partitioning the array into groups in L2. Connection to L4: It relies on iterative addition from L4. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or not handling empty segments. Real code pattern: Define a function sum_segment(pages, start, end) that loops and accumulates.

examples.trace: Sum from index 1 to 2: pages[1]=20, pages[2]=30, total = 20+30 = 50.
Sum from index 0 to 0: only pages[0]=5, sum=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Summation of Array Elements
ladders.desc: Add up numbers in a list using a loop.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental programming operation: summing a sequence of numbers. The intuition is that to find the total of multiple values, we start with zero and add each value one by one. In the context of the book allocation problem, we use this to compute the total pages for a set of books. The step-by-step algorithm: Initialize a variable total to 0. Iterate through each element in the array (or a specified range). For each element, add its value to total. After the loop, total holds the sum. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space. Connection to L3: This operation is the core of computing the sum of a contiguous subarray in L3. Connection to L5: It builds upon the concept of addition from L5. Common mistakes include not initializing total to 0, off-by-one errors in loops, or using the wrong variable. Real code pattern: In Python-style, total = 0; for num in arr: total += num.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. First element 5: total=0+5=5. Second element 10: total=5+10=15. Third element 15: total=15+15=30. Final total=30.
total=0. Add 2: total=2. Add 4: total=6. Add 6: total=12. Add 8: total=20.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Addition of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Basic arithmetic operation of adding two integers.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: adding two numbers together. In programming, addition is a fundamental operation used in many algorithms, including summing arrays. The intuition is that addition combines quantities; for example, if you have a pages and b pages, the total is a + b. In the context of the book allocation problem, we use addition repeatedly to compute sums of page counts. The step-by-step process: Take two numbers, say a and b. Their sum is a + b. In pseudocode, this is simply result = a + b. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a constant-time operation. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L4: Addition is the building block for iterative summation in L4. Common mistakes include integer overflow for large numbers, but in basic learning, we assume standard integer handling. Real code pattern: In Python, c = a + b.

examples.trace: Add 5 and 3: 5 + 3 = 8.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2 * k)
approaches.summary: Use DP where dp[i][j] stores the minimum maximum pages for i books and j students, building solutions from subproblems. Apply this for a systematic, non-heuristic approach that guarantees optimality but is slower than binary search for large n and k.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Allocate Minimum Pages Using Dynamic Programming
ladders.desc: Complete DP solution to allocate books minimizing maximum pages per student
ladders.explanation: This is the complete DP solution to the book allocation problem. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible ways to partition n books among k students using dynamic programming. We define dp[i][j] as the minimum possible maximum pages when allocating the first i books to j students. The recurrence relation is: dp[i][j] = min_{x=1 to i-1}(max(dp[x][j-1], sum(pages[x+1...i]))). This means for i books and j students, we try all possible positions x where the last student gets books from x+1 to i, and the first j-1 students get books 1 through x. We take the minimum of the maximum pages among these partitions. The base cases are: dp[i][1] = sum of first i books (all books to one student), and dp[1][j] = pages[1] (one book to any number of students). The final answer is dp[n][k]. The algorithm builds the DP table bottom-up, filling for increasing i and j. Time complexity is O(n² * k) due to three nested loops: i (1 to n), j (2 to k), and x (1 to i-1). Space complexity is O(n * k) for the DP table. This approach guarantees optimality by exploring all partitions but becomes inefficient for large n and k compared to binary search. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, off-by-one errors in indexing, and not handling cases where k > n (impossible allocation). The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the prefix sum calculation and DP recurrence into a complete solution.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence for Partition Optimization
ladders.desc: Implement the recurrence relation to compute optimal partitions
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core recurrence relation of the DP solution: dp[i][j] = min_{x=1 to i-1}(max(dp[x][j-1], sum(pages[x+1...i]))). The intuition is that to allocate i books to j students optimally, we consider all possible splits where the last student gets books from position x+1 to i, and the remaining j-1 students get books 1 to x. We want to minimize the maximum pages any student receives, so we take the minimum over all splits of the maximum between the optimal allocation for the first x books to j-1 students and the pages given to the last student. The algorithm involves iterating x from 1 to i-1, computing the sum of pages from x+1 to i (which can be done efficiently with prefix sums), and updating dp[i][j] with the minimum max value. Time complexity for computing one dp[i][j] is O(i) due to the loop over x, and overall O(n² * k) when integrated. Space is O(1) per computation plus the DP table. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing in the sum (off-by-one), not initializing dp[i][j] to a large value before minimization, and missing the case where x ranges up to i-1 (since at least one book must go to the last student). The connection to L0 is that this recurrence is applied repeatedly in the full solution to fill the DP table. The connection to L2 is that this builds upon the prefix sum technique to compute sums quickly.

examples.trace: For i=3, j=2. Initialize dp[3][2]=inf. Loop x from 1 to 2: x=1: max(dp[1][1], sum(pages[2..3])) = max(10, 20+30=50) = 50. x=2: max(dp[2][1], sum(pages[3..3])) = max(30, 30) = 30. Update dp[3][2] = min(50, 30) = 30.

testCases.input: dp[1][1]=5, dp[2][1]=15, pages=[5,10], prefix sums=[5,15], compute dp[2][2]
dp[1][1]=0, dp[2][1]=5, pages=[0,5], prefix sums=[0,5], compute dp[2][2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix Sum Array for Efficient Range Sum Queries
ladders.desc: Compute and use prefix sums to get sum of any subarray in O(1) time
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the prefix sum technique, which is essential for efficiently computing the sum of pages for any contiguous segment of books in O(1) time. The intuition is that by precomputing cumulative sums, we can answer sum queries for ranges [l, r] instantly as prefix[r] - prefix[l-1], where prefix[i] is the sum of the first i elements. This avoids O(n) time per query in the DP recurrence. The algorithm: given an array pages of length n, compute prefix array where prefix[0]=0 and prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+pages[i] for i from 1 to n. Then, sum(pages[l...r]) = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. Time complexity for preprocessing is O(n), and each query is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for the prefix array. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (e.g., using 0-based vs 1-based), not handling empty ranges, and off-by-one errors in the subtraction. The connection to L1 is that this technique is used to compute sum(pages[x+1...i]) quickly in the DP recurrence. The connection to L3 is that this builds upon basic array summation.

examples.input: pages = [10, 20, 30, 40], compute sum from index 2 to 4 (1-based)
examples.trace: Compute prefix array: prefix[0]=0, prefix[1]=10, prefix[2]=30, prefix[3]=60, prefix[4]=100. Sum(pages[2...4]) = prefix[4] - prefix[1] = 100 - 10 = 90.

examples.input: pages = [12, 34, 67, 90], compute sum from index 1 to 3 (1-based)
examples.trace: Prefix array: prefix[0]=0, prefix[1]=12, prefix[2]=46, prefix[3]=113, prefix[4]=203. Sum(pages[1...3]) = prefix[3] - prefix[0] = 113 - 0 = 113.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming Table Initialization and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Set up DP table with base cases for book allocation
ladders.explanation: This level involves initializing the DP table and defining base cases for the allocation problem. The intuition is that we need to handle edge scenarios: when there's only one student, all books must be given to that student, so dp[i][1] is the sum of the first i books. When there's only one book, regardless of the number of students, that book's pages are the maximum, so dp[1][j] = pages[1]. The DP table dp is of size (n+1) x (k+1) to handle 1-based indexing, initialized with a large value (e.g., infinity) except for base cases. The algorithm: create dp array with dimensions (n+1) x (k+1), fill all with a large number. For i from 1 to n: dp[i][1] = sum of pages[1...i] (computed using prefix sums). For j from 1 to k: dp[1][j] = pages[1]. Time complexity is O(n + k) for initialization, and space is O(n * k). Common mistakes include incorrect dimensions (off-by-one), not using a large enough initial value, and mishandling cases where k > n (which should be handled separately as impossible). The connection to L2 is that prefix sums are used to compute dp[i][1]. The connection to L4 is that this involves basic array operations and loops.

examples.trace: n=3, k=3. Initialize dp[4][4] with inf. Compute prefix sums: [10,30,60]. Set dp[i][1]: dp[1][1]=10, dp[2][1]=30, dp[3][1]=60. Set dp[1][j]: dp[1][1]=10, dp[1][2]=10, dp[1][3]=10. Rest remain inf.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Possible Split Points in an Array
ladders.desc: Loop through indices to explore all ways to split an array into two parts
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic operation of iterating over possible split points in an array, which is used in the DP recurrence to try all partitions. The intuition is that to split an array of length i into two contiguous parts, we can place the split after any index x from 1 to i-1, resulting in a left part [1...x] and a right part [x+1...i]. This explores all possible divisions. The algorithm involves a simple loop: for x in range(1, i): left_part = array[1...x], right_part = array[x+1...i]. In the context of book allocation, this corresponds to giving the first x books to some students and the remaining books to another student. Time complexity is O(i) for splitting an array of length i, and space is O(1) if not storing the parts. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., x from 0 or up to i), off-by-one errors in array slicing, and not handling the case where i=1 (no valid splits). The connection to L3 is that this operation is used in filling the DP table by trying all x. The connection to L5 is that it builds upon basic loop constructs.

examples.output: Splits: x=1: left=[10], right=[20,30]; x=2: left=[10,20], right=[30]
examples.trace: For i=3, loop x from 1 to 2: x=1: left part indices 1 to 1: [10], right part indices 2 to 3: [20,30]. x=2: left part indices 1 to 2: [10,20], right part indices 3 to 3: [30].

testCases.expected: x=1: left=[1], right=[2,3]; x=2: left=[1,2], right=[3]
x=1: left=[7], right=[8,9]; x=2: left=[7,8], right=[9]
x=1: left=[2], right=[3,4]; x=2: left=[2,3], right=[4]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summing Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Compute the total sum of elements in an array or a subarray
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept of summing elements in an array, which is essential for calculating the total pages in books. The intuition is simple: to find the total, we add all elements together. For a subarray from index l to r (1-based), we sum only those elements. The algorithm involves initializing a variable total to 0, then iterating through the array or the specified range, adding each element to total. Time complexity is O(n) for summing n elements, and space is O(1). Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds, not initializing total to 0, and using the wrong indices for subarrays. This concept is a prerequisite for more advanced operations like prefix sums. The connection to L4 is that summing is used to compute the pages for the right part in splits. The connection to higher levels is that efficient summing via prefix sums optimizes the DP solution.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. Add 10: total=10. Add 20: total=30. Add 30: total=60.
Indices 2 to 4: elements 34, 67, 90. Total=0. Add 34: total=34. Add 67: total=101. Add 90: total=191.

approaches.name: Binary Search on Answer O(n log S)
approaches.summary: Binary search over the possible maximum page values (from max page to total pages), using a greedy helper function to check if a given maximum is feasible by allocating books. This is the optimal approach for large inputs, leveraging monotonicity to efficiently narrow down the answer.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log S) where S is sum of pages

ladders.title: Full Problem — Allocate Minimum Pages Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search on answer with greedy feasibility check
ladders.explanation: This problem requires allocating n books with given page counts to m students such that each student gets at least one book, books are allocated in contiguous order, and the maximum pages allocated to any student is minimized. The optimal approach uses binary search over possible maximum page values. The intuition is that if we can allocate books with maximum pages X, we can also allocate with any maximum > X (monotonic property). We binary search between max(pages) (minimum possible answer) and sum(pages) (maximum possible answer). For each mid value, we use a greedy helper function to check if we can allocate all books to m students without exceeding mid pages per student. If feasible, we try smaller values; otherwise, we try larger values. Time complexity is O(n log S) where S is sum of pages, and space is O(1). The greedy check works because we allocate as many books as possible to each student without exceeding the limit, which is optimal for checking feasibility. Common mistakes include incorrect binary search bounds, off-by-one errors in allocation, and not handling cases where m > n. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the binary search framework with the feasibility check from L1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize low = max(pages) = 90, high = sum(pages) = 203. Step 2: Binary search: mid = (90+203)//2 = 146. Check feasibility with max 146: Student1 gets 12+34+67=113, Student2 gets 90 → feasible (students=2). Update high = 146. Step 3: mid = (90+146)//2 = 118. Check: Student1 gets 12+34+67=113, Student2 gets 90 → feasible. Update high = 118. Step 4: mid = (90+118)//2 = 104. Check: Student1 gets 12+34=46, Student2 gets 67 → exceeds 104? No, but Student3 would need 90 → requires 3 students, not feasible for m=2. Update low = 105. Step 5: mid = (105+118)//2 = 111. Check: Student1 gets 12+34+67=113 → exceeds 111? Yes, so split: Student1 gets 12+34=46, Student2 gets 67, Student3 gets 90 → 3 students, not feasible. Update low = 112. Step 6: mid = (112+118)//2 = 115. Check: Student1 gets 12+34+67=113, Student2 gets 90 → feasible. Update high = 115. Step 7: mid = (112+115)//2 = 113. Check: Student1 gets 12+34+67=113, Student2 gets 90 → feasible. Update high = 113. Step 8: low=112, high=113, low<high? Yes. mid = (112+113)//2 = 112. Check: Student1 gets 12+34+67=113 → exceeds 112, so split: Student1 gets 12+34=46, Student2 gets 67, Student3 gets 90 → 3 students, not feasible. Update low = 113. Now low=113, high=113, loop ends. Return 113.

Step 1: low = max(pages)=40, high = sum(pages)=100. Step 2: mid = (40+100)//2=70. Check feasibility: Student1 gets 10+20+30=60, Student2 gets 40 → 2 students used, feasible for m=3. Update high=70. Step 3: mid = (40+70)//2=55. Check: Student1 gets 10+20+30=60 → exceeds 55, so split: Student1 gets 10+20=30, Student2 gets 30, Student3 gets 40 → 3 students, feasible. Update high=55. Step 4: mid = (40+55)//2=47. Check: Student1 gets 10+20=30, Student2 gets 30, Student3 gets 40 → 3 students, feasible. Update high=47. Step 5: mid = (40+47)//2=43. Check: Student1 gets 10+20=30, Student2 gets 30, Student3 gets 40 → 3 students, feasible. Update high=43. Step 6: mid = (40+43)//2=41. Check: Student1 gets 10+20=30, Student2 gets 30, Student3 gets 40 → 3 students, feasible. Update high=41. Step 7: mid = (40+41)//2=40. Check: Student1 gets 10+20=30, Student2 gets 30, Student3 gets 40 → 3 students, feasible. Update high=40. Now low=40, high=40, loop ends. But wait, verify: With max=40, allocation: Student1: 10+20=30, Student2: 30, Student3: 40 → works. But is this minimal? Check lower: max=39? Not possible since max page book is 40. So answer is 40? Let's re-check binary search: After step 7, high=40, low=40, return 40. But expected output is 60? Let's trace carefully: With max=40, Student1 gets 10+20=30 (<=40), Student2 gets 30 (<=40), Student3 gets 40 (<=40) → feasible. But is this optimal? The problem minimizes maximum pages; here max is 40. But can we do better? No, because one book has 40 pages, so maximum must be at least 40. So answer should be 40. But wait, the example says output 60? Let's re-read: For pages [10,20,30,40] and m=3, the optimal allocation is Student1: 10+20=30, Student2: 30, Student3: 40, so maximum is 40. But maybe I misinterpreted: The example input says output 60, but that seems incorrect. Let's compute manually: If we allocate as [10,20], [30], [40], max is 40. If we allocate as [10], [20,30], [40], max is 50. If [10], [20], [30,40], max is 70. So minimum max is 40. So the example output should be 40. But the instruction says output 60, which is wrong. I'll correct: Output is 40. Trace: Binary search as above gives 40. So final trace yields 40.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Greedy Feasibility Check
ladders.desc: Check if books can be allocated to m students with a given maximum pages per student
ladders.explanation: This helper function determines whether it's possible to allocate all books to exactly m students such that no student receives more than a specified maximum page limit. The intuition is greedy: we allocate books sequentially to students, giving each student as many books as possible without exceeding the limit, then move to the next student. This works because if we can allocate with this greedy method, a feasible allocation exists; if not, no allocation exists for that limit. The algorithm: Initialize student count = 1 and current pages = 0. Iterate through each book: if current pages + book pages <= limit, add to current student; else, start a new student with this book and increment student count. If at any point student count exceeds m, return false; after processing all books, return true if student count <= m. Time complexity is O(n), space O(1). Common mistakes include not resetting current pages correctly or miscounting students. This feeds into L0 by being the check used in binary search. Real code pattern: def is_feasible(pages, m, limit): students=1, curr=0; for p in pages: if curr+p <= limit: curr+=p; else: students+=1; curr=p; if students>m: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: students=1, curr=0. Book 12: curr+12=12 <=113 → curr=12. Step 2: Book 34: curr+34=46 <=113 → curr=46. Step 3: Book 67: curr+67=113 <=113 → curr=113. Step 4: Book 90: curr+90=203 >113 → start new student: students=2, curr=90. Step 5: All books processed, students=2 <= m=2 → return True.

Step 1: students=1, curr=0. Book 10: curr+10=10 <=40 → curr=10. Step 2: Book 20: curr+20=30 <=40 → curr=30. Step 3: Book 30: curr+30=60 >40 → start new student: students=2, curr=30. Step 4: Book 40: curr+40=70 >40 → start new student: students=3, curr=40. Step 5: All books processed, students=3 <= m=3 → return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Answer with Monotonicity
ladders.desc: Using binary search to find minimal feasible maximum pages by exploiting monotonic property
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that the feasibility function is monotonic: if a maximum page limit X is feasible (books can be allocated to m students), then any limit greater than X is also feasible. Conversely, if X is not feasible, any limit less than X is also not feasible. This monotonicity allows us to use binary search to efficiently find the minimal feasible limit. We search over the range [max(pages), sum(pages)], where the lower bound is the largest single book (since at least one student must get that book) and the upper bound is the total pages (if one student gets all books). For each mid value, we check feasibility using the helper from L1. If feasible, we try smaller limits by moving high to mid; if not feasible, we try larger limits by moving low to mid+1. We continue until low equals high, which gives the minimal feasible limit. Time complexity is O(n log S) due to binary search over range size S and O(n) checks. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include incorrect binary search termination or bounds. This feeds into L0 as the main algorithm framework. Real code pattern: low, high = max(pages), sum(pages); while low < high: mid = (low+high)//2; if is_feasible(pages, m, mid): high = mid; else: low = mid+1; return low.

examples.trace: Step 1: low=50, high=200. Step 2: mid=(50+200)//2=125. Check feasibility with limit 125: Greedy allocation: Student1 gets 50+50=100, Student2 gets 50+50=100 → 2 students, feasible. Update high=125. Step 3: mid=(50+125)//2=87. Check limit 87: Student1 gets 50+50=100 >87 → split: Student1 gets 50, Student2 gets 50, Student3 gets 50, Student4 gets 50 → 4 students, not feasible for m=2. Update low=88. Step 4: mid=(88+125)//2=106. Check limit 106: Student1 gets 50+50=100, Student2 gets 50+50=100 → feasible. Update high=106. Step 5: mid=(88+106)//2=97. Check limit 97: Student1 gets 50+50=100 >97 → split: 4 students, not feasible. Update low=98. Step 6: mid=(98+106)//2=102. Check limit 102: Student1 gets 50+50=100, Student2 gets 50+50=100 → feasible. Update high=102. Step 7: mid=(98+102)//2=100. Check limit 100: Student1 gets 50+50=100, Student2 gets 50+50=100 → feasible. Update high=100. Step 8: low=98, high=100, low<high? Yes. mid=(98+100)//2=99. Check limit 99: Student1 gets 50+50=100 >99 → split: 4 students, not feasible. Update low=100. Now low=100, high=100, return 100.

Step 1: low=30, high=60. Step 2: mid=(30+60)//2=45. Check limit 45: Greedy: Student1 gets 30+20=50 >45 → split: Student1 gets 30, Student2 gets 20, Student3 gets 10 → 3 students, not feasible for m=2. Update low=46. Step 3: mid=(46+60)//2=53. Check limit 53: Student1 gets 30+20=50, Student2 gets 10 → 2 students, feasible. Update high=53. Step 4: mid=(46+53)//2=49. Check limit 49: Student1 gets 30+20=50 >49 → split: 3 students, not feasible. Update low=50. Step 5: mid=(50+53)//2=51. Check limit 51: Student1 gets 30+20=50, Student2 gets 10 → feasible. Update high=51. Step 6: mid=(50+51)//2=50. Check limit 50: Student1 gets 30+20=50, Student2 gets 10 → feasible. Update high=50. Now low=50, high=50, return 50? But wait, let's verify: With limit 50, allocation: Student1: 30+20=50, Student2: 10 → max is 50. But is 40 possible? Check limit 40: Student1 gets 30+10=40, Student2 gets 20 → max 40, feasible? Yes: Student1: 30+10=40, Student2: 20. So 40 is feasible and less than 50. My binary search missed this because I started with low=30, but after step 4, low=50. Let's re-trace carefully: Initial: pages sorted? Not needed, but allocation must be contiguous. With pages [30,20,10] in order, for limit 40: Student1 takes 30 (<=40), next book 20: 30+20=50 >40, so start new student with 20? But books are contiguous, so Student1 gets 30, Student2 gets 20+10=30 → max 30? Wait, that gives max 30, even better. But with greedy: Student1: 30 (<=40), Student2: 20 (<=40), now current for Student2 is 20, next book 10: 20+10=30 <=40, so Student2 gets 10 as well → Student2 has 30. So allocation: Student1:30, Student2:30, max=30. So minimal max is 30, not 40 or 50. My earlier trace was wrong. Let's do binary search correctly: low=30, high=60. mid=45: Check limit 45: Greedy: Student1: 30 (<=45), next 20: 30+20=50 >45 → start Student2 with 20, next 10: 20+10=30 <=45 → Student2 gets 10. So students=2, feasible. Update high=45. mid=(30+45)//2=37. Check limit 37: Student1: 30, next 20: 30+20=50 >37 → start Student2 with 20, next 10: 20+10=30 <=37 → Student2 gets 10. Students=2, feasible. Update high=37. mid=(30+37)//2=33. Check limit 33: Student1: 30, next 20: 30+20=50 >33 → start Student2 with 20, next 10: 20+10=30 <=33 → Student2 gets 10. Students=2, feasible. Update high=33. mid=(30+33)//2=31. Check limit 31: Student1: 30, next 20: 30+20=50 >31 → start Student2 with 20, next 10: 20+10=30 <=31 → Student2 gets 10. Students=2, feasible. Update high=31. mid=(30+31)//2=30. Check limit 30: Student1: 30, next 20: 30+20=50 >30 → start Student2 with 20, next 10: 20+10=30 <=30 → Student2 gets 10. Students=2, feasible. Update high=30. Now low=30, high=30, return 30. So output is 30. I'll correct the example.

ladders.title: Building Block — Monotonic Function and Search Space Reduction
ladders.desc: Understanding monotonicity in feasibility and defining search bounds for binary search
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the concept that the feasibility function f(limit) returns True if books can be allocated with that maximum limit, and False otherwise. The key insight is that f is monotonic: if f(X) is True, then for any Y > X, f(Y) is also True because a higher limit makes allocation easier. Similarly, if f(X) is False, then for any Y < X, f(Y) is also False because a lower limit makes allocation harder. This monotonic property allows binary search to efficiently find the smallest X where f(X) is True. The search space is defined by lower and upper bounds: the lower bound is max(pages) because at least one student must get the largest book, so the maximum cannot be less than that. The upper bound is sum(pages) because in the worst case, one student gets all books. These bounds ensure the answer lies within this range. Time complexity for defining bounds is O(n) to compute max and sum. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include setting incorrect bounds, e.g., using 0 as lower bound. This feeds into L2 by providing the foundation for binary search. Real code pattern: low = max(pages); high = sum(pages).

examples.trace: Step 1: Find max(pages): iterate through [12,34,67,90], max is 90. Step 2: Find sum(pages): 12+34+67+90 = 203. Step 3: Therefore, search range is [90, 203].
Step 1: max(pages) = 40. Step 2: sum(pages) = 10+20+30+40 = 100. Step 3: Search range [40, 100].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Greedy Allocation
ladders.desc: Allocating items sequentially with a capacity constraint
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating through a list of items (e.g., book pages) and grouping them into containers (e.g., students) such that the total in each container does not exceed a given capacity. The greedy approach is to add items to the current container as long as the total does not exceed the capacity; when adding the next item would exceed the capacity, start a new container with that item. This is a basic bin packing or partitioning problem. The intuition is that by filling each container as much as possible, we minimize the number of containers used for a fixed capacity. The algorithm: Initialize container count = 1 and current total = 0. For each item: if current total + item <= capacity, add item to current container; else, increment container count and start a new container with this item. After processing all items, return the container count. Time complexity is O(n), space O(1). Common mistakes include not updating current total correctly or miscounting containers. This feeds into L1 as the core of the feasibility check. Real code pattern: def allocate_items(items, capacity): count=1, curr=0; for item in items: if curr+item <= capacity: curr+=item; else: count+=1; curr=item; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: count=1, curr=0. Item 12: curr+12=12 <=100 → curr=12. Step 2: Item 34: curr+34=46 <=100 → curr=46. Step 3: Item 67: curr+67=113 >100 → start new container: count=2, curr=67. Step 4: Item 90: curr+90=157 >100 → start new container: count=3, curr=90. Step 5: Return count=3.

Step 1: count=1, curr=0. Item 10: curr+10=10 <=50 → curr=10. Step 2: Item 20: curr+20=30 <=50 → curr=30. Step 3: Item 30: curr+30=60 >50 → start new container: count=2, curr=30. Step 4: Item 40: curr+40=70 >50 → start new container: count=3, curr=40. Wait, that gives 3 containers, but output says 2. Let's re-trace: With capacity 50, after Step 3, we have count=2, curr=30. Step 4: Item 40: curr+40=70 >50, so start new container: count=3, curr=40. So containers=3. But maybe the example expects 2? Check: If we allocate as [10,20,30] and [40], that's 2 containers? But [10,20,30] sums to 60 >50, so not allowed. So with capacity 50, minimal containers is 3: [10,20], [30], [40]. So output should be 3. I'll correct the example.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding binary search on a sorted range to find a target value
ladders.explanation: Binary search is a fundamental algorithm used to efficiently find a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is that if the array is sorted, we can compare the middle element with the target and eliminate half of the remaining elements based on the comparison. This reduces the time complexity from O(n) for linear search to O(log n). The basic algorithm: Initialize low and high indices to the start and end of the array. While low <= high, compute mid = (low + high) // 2. If the element at mid equals the target, return mid. If it is less than the target, set low = mid + 1 to search the right half. If it is greater, set high = mid - 1 to search the left half. If the loop ends without finding the target, return -1 or an appropriate value. Time complexity is O(log n), space O(1). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or incorrect termination conditions. This feeds into L2 as the underlying technique for searching over a range of values. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=4. mid=(0+4)//2=2. arr[2]=5 equals target → return 2.
Step 1: low=0, high=4. mid=2, arr[2]=6 <7 → low=3. Step 2: low=3, high=4. mid=3, arr[3]=8 >7 → high=2. Step 3: low=3, high=2, low>high → loop ends, return -1.
